title: Goldbach: sum of two primes
description: Count ways to write even N as sum of two primes

examples.explanation: N=10 (even, ≥4). Initialize count=0. Loop i from 2 to 5: i=2: is_prime(2)=True, is_prime(8)=False → no count. i=3: is_prime(3)=True, is_prime(7)=True → count=1. i=4: is_prime(4)=False → skip. i=5: is_prime(5)=True, is_prime(5)=True → count=2. Return 2. Representations: 3+7, 5+5.

N=20. count=0. i=2: 2 prime, 18 not prime. i=3: 3 prime, 17 prime → count=1. i=4: 4 not prime. i=5: 5 prime, 15 not prime. i=6: 6 not prime. i=7: 7 prime, 13 prime → count=2. i=8: 8 not prime. i=9: 9 not prime. i=10: 10 not prime. Return 4? Wait, need to continue: Actually i up to 10. i=11 not in loop (N/2=10). Let's recalc properly: Loop i=2 to 10: i=2: 2✓, 18✗. i=3: 3✓, 17✓ → count=1. i=4: 4✗. i=5: 5✓, 15✗. i=6: 6✗. i=7: 7✓, 13✓ → count=2. i=8: 8✗. i=9: 9✗. i=10: 10✗. That gives 2, but expected 4. Error in example? Let me compute correctly: For N=20, prime pairs: (3,17), (7,13), (13,7), (17,3) but we only count i≤N/2 so (3,17) and (7,13) → 2. Actually 20 has representations: 3+17, 7+13. That's 2. But my earlier output said 4 - that was wrong. Let me fix: Output should be 2. Trace: i=2:2 prime,18 not. i=3:3 prime,17 prime→count=1. i=4:4 not. i=5:5 prime,15 not. i=6:6 not. i=7:7 prime,13 prime→count=2. i=8,9,10 not prime. Return 2.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Iterate through all pairs (i, N-i) for i from 2 to N/2, and check if both i and N-i are prime using a primality test for each pair. This is straightforward but inefficient for large N.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Goldbach Representations with Brute Force
ladders.desc: Complete implementation counting ways to express even N as sum of two primes using O(n²) brute force approach.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete Goldbach conjecture counting problem solved via brute force. The intuition is straightforward: for an even number N, we need to find all pairs (p, q) where both p and q are prime numbers and p + q = N. Since N is even and we want unique representations (p ≤ q), we only need to check i from 2 to N/2. For each candidate i, we check if both i and N-i are prime using a primality test. The brute force nature comes from checking every possible i in the range, making it O(n²) when combined with primality testing. Step-by-step: 1) Validate input is even and ≥4. 2) Initialize count = 0. 3) For i from 2 to N/2: a) If is_prime(i) and is_prime(N-i): count++. 4) Return count. Time complexity is O(n² * sqrt(n)) because we have O(n) iterations, each performing two primality tests that are O(sqrt(n)). Space is O(1). This connects directly to L1's primality test function - we use that helper repeatedly. Common mistakes include: not handling edge cases (N=2, odd N), double-counting pairs, or inefficient primality tests. Real code pattern: def count_goldbach(N): if N%2!=0 or N<4: return 0; count=0; for i in range(2, N//2+1): if is_prime(i) and is_prime(N-i): count+=1; return count.

examples.trace: N=10 (even, ≥4). Initialize count=0. Loop i from 2 to 5: i=2: is_prime(2)=True, is_prime(8)=False → no count. i=3: is_prime(3)=True, is_prime(7)=True → count=1. i=4: is_prime(4)=False → skip. i=5: is_prime(5)=True, is_prime(5)=True → count=2. Return 2. Representations: 3+7, 5+5.

N=20. count=0. i=2: 2 prime, 18 not prime. i=3: 3 prime, 17 prime → count=1. i=4: 4 not prime. i=5: 5 prime, 15 not prime. i=6: 6 not prime. i=7: 7 prime, 13 prime → count=2. i=8: 8 not prime. i=9: 9 not prime. i=10: 10 not prime. Return 4? Wait, need to continue: Actually i up to 10. i=11 not in loop (N/2=10). Let's recalc properly: Loop i=2 to 10: i=2: 2✓, 18✗. i=3: 3✓, 17✓ → count=1. i=4: 4✗. i=5: 5✓, 15✗. i=6: 6✗. i=7: 7✓, 13✓ → count=2. i=8: 8✗. i=9: 9✗. i=10: 10✗. That gives 2, but expected 4. Error in example? Let me compute correctly: For N=20, prime pairs: (3,17), (7,13), (13,7), (17,3) but we only count i≤N/2 so (3,17) and (7,13) → 2. Actually 20 has representations: 3+17, 7+13. That's 2. But my earlier output said 4 - that was wrong. Let me fix: Output should be 2. Trace: i=2:2 prime,18 not. i=3:3 prime,17 prime→count=1. i=4:4 not. i=5:5 prime,15 not. i=6:6 not. i=7:7 prime,13 prime→count=2. i=8,9,10 not prime. Return 2.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Primality Test Function
ladders.desc: Implement is_prime(n) function to check if a number is prime using trial division up to sqrt(n).
ladders.explanation: This is the critical helper function for the Goldbach problem. The intuition: a prime number is divisible only by 1 and itself, so we can test divisibility by all integers from 2 up to sqrt(n). Why sqrt(n)? Because if n has a divisor d > sqrt(n), then n/d would be < sqrt(n) and we would have already found it. Step-by-step: 1) Handle edge cases: n < 2 returns False. 2) For i from 2 to int(sqrt(n)): if n % i == 0, return False. 3) Return True. Time complexity is O(sqrt(n)) as we iterate up to sqrt(n). Space is O(1). This feeds directly into L0 where we call is_prime() for both i and N-i. Common mistakes include: not handling n=0,1,2; using range up to n-1 (inefficient); or not converting sqrt to integer properly. Real code pattern: import math; def is_prime(n): if n<2: return False; for i in range(2, int(math.sqrt(n))+1): if n%i==0: return False; return True.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterating Through Candidate Pairs
ladders.desc: Generate all candidate pairs (i, N-i) for i from 2 to N/2 to find prime sums.
ladders.explanation: This is the core algorithmic logic of the brute force approach. The intuition: since we need p + q = N with p ≤ q (to avoid double-counting), we can fix p as i and q as N-i, with i ranging from 2 to N/2. Why start at 2? Because 1 is not prime. Why go to N/2? Because when i > N/2, we'd get duplicate pairs (e.g., for N=10, i=7 gives pair (7,3) which is same as i=3's (3,7)). Step-by-step: 1) Given even N ≥4. 2) For i in range(2, N//2 + 1): generate pair (i, N-i). 3) This produces all unique candidate pairs. Time complexity is O(n) for the iteration. Space is O(1). This connects to L1 by needing to test primality of each i and N-i. Common mistakes include: starting i from 1 (1 not prime), going beyond N/2 (duplicates), or incorrect loop bounds. Real code pattern: for i in range(2, N//2 + 1): p = i; q = N - i; # then check if both prime.

ladders.title: Building Block — Divisibility Check and Loop to sqrt(n)
ladders.desc: Check if a number is divisible by any integer from 2 to sqrt(n) using modulo operation.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is essential for primality testing. The intuition: to determine if n has any divisors other than 1 and itself, we test divisibility by numbers up to sqrt(n). We use the modulo operator (%) which gives remainder. If n % i == 0, then i divides n evenly. Why only up to sqrt(n)? Mathematical property: if n = a*b, then at least one of a or b is ≤ sqrt(n). So checking up to sqrt(n) is sufficient. Step-by-step: 1) Compute limit = int(sqrt(n)). 2) For i in range(2, limit+1): if n % i == 0, then n is composite. 3) If loop completes without finding divisor, n is prime. Time O(sqrt(n)), space O(1). This builds toward L2's primality test. Common mistakes include: off-by-one in range, not handling integer conversion of sqrt, or not understanding why sqrt(n) is enough. Real code pattern: limit = int(n**0.5); for i in range(2, limit+1): if n%i==0: return False.

examples.input: n = 36, check divisibility up to sqrt(36)=6
examples.trace: n=36, sqrt=6. i=2: 36%2=0 → divisible, return composite immediately.

examples.input: n = 37, check divisibility up to sqrt(37)≈6

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Modulo and Integer Division
ladders.desc: Use modulo operator (%) to check divisibility and integer division (//) for bounds.
ladders.explanation: These fundamental operations are building blocks for the algorithm. Modulo (n % d) returns remainder when n is divided by d. If remainder is 0, n is divisible by d. Integer division (n // d) returns quotient without remainder, useful for computing N/2 bound. Intuition: modulo helps test divisibility in primality check; integer division helps set loop bounds. Step-by-step examples: 10 % 3 = 1 (not divisible), 10 % 2 = 0 (divisible). 10 // 2 = 5 (half). Time O(1), space O(1). This feeds into L3's divisibility checking. Common mistakes include: confusing / (float division) with // (integer division), or misunderstanding modulo with negative numbers. Real code pattern: divisible = (n % d == 0); half = N // 2.

examples.input: Check if 15 divisible by 3 and 4, compute 15//2
examples.trace: 15 % 3 = 0 → divisible by 3. 15 % 4 = 3 → not divisible by 4. 15 // 2 = 7 (since 2*7=14, remainder 1).

examples.input: Check if 28 divisible by 7 and 9, compute 28//2
examples.trace: 28 % 7 = 0 → divisible. 28 % 9 = 1 → not divisible. 28 // 2 = 14.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Even Numbers and Basic Loops
ladders.desc: Identify even numbers and iterate with for-loops from start to end.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: understanding even numbers and basic iteration. An even number is divisible by 2 (N % 2 == 0). For-loops allow repeating operations for a range of values. Intuition: Goldbach applies only to even numbers, so we need to check if N is even. Loops let us test multiple candidate values. Step-by-step: 1) Check evenness: if N % 2 == 0. 2) Basic for-loop: for i in range(start, end): # end is exclusive. 3) Example: for i in range(2, 6): processes i=2,3,4,5. Time O(n) for loop, space O(1). This foundation supports L4's operations. Common mistakes include: off-by-one errors in range, misunderstanding even/odd, or infinite loops. Real code pattern: if N % 2 == 0: print('even'); for i in range(2, N//2 + 1): print(i).

examples.input: Check if 14 is even, loop i from 2 to 7
Check if 15 is even, loop i from 2 to 7
examples.output: 15 is odd, loop would still run but problem requires even N
examples.trace: 15 % 2 = 1 → odd. But if we compute 15//2=7, range(2,8) still gives 2,3,4,5,6,7. However for Goldbach we first check evenness.

approaches.name: Sieve of Eratosthenes O(n log log n)
approaches.summary: Use the Sieve of Eratosthenes to generate a boolean array of primes up to N, then iterate i from 2 to N/2 and count pairs where both i and N-i are prime based on the sieve. This precomputes primes for efficient lookups.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Goldbach Pairs Using Sieve of Eratosthenes
ladders.desc: Complete solution to count ways to write even N as sum of two primes using Sieve of Eratosthenes for prime generation.
ladders.explanation: This solution solves the Goldbach conjecture counting problem efficiently by precomputing all primes up to N using the Sieve of Eratosthenes, then counting valid prime pairs. The intuition is that checking primality repeatedly for each candidate pair would be inefficient (O(n√n)), but by generating a boolean prime array once (O(n log log n)), we can check pairs in O(n) time with simple array lookups. The algorithm first creates a boolean array is_prime where is_prime[i] = True if i is prime. Using the Sieve: mark all numbers as prime initially, set 0 and 1 as non-prime, then for each i from 2 to √n, if i is prime, mark all multiples of i as non-prime. After generating primes, iterate i from 2 to N/2, and for each i, if both i and N-i are prime (checked via is_prime array), increment count. Return count. Time complexity is O(n log log n) for sieve + O(n) for counting = O(n log log n). Space is O(n) for the boolean array. Connection to L1: This uses the sieve generation (L1) as a key subroutine. Common mistakes include: not handling N=2 or N=4 correctly (should return 0), counting pairs twice (i and N-i vs N-i and i), or not optimizing sieve to √n. Real code pattern: def count_goldbach_pairs(N): if N < 4 or N % 2 != 0: return 0; is_prime = [True]*(N+1); is_prime[0]=is_prime[1]=False; for i in range(2, int(N**0.5)+1): if is_prime[i]: for j in range(i*i, N+1, i): is_prime[j]=False; count=0; for i in range(2, N//2+1): if is_prime[i] and is_prime[N-i]: count+=1; return count.

examples.trace: Step 1: N=10 is even and ≥4, proceed. Step 2: Generate primes up to 10 using Sieve. Initialize is_prime = [True]*11. Set is_prime[0]=False, is_prime[1]=False. i=2: is_prime[2]=True, mark multiples: 4,6,8,10 as False. i=3: is_prime[3]=True, mark multiples: 6,9 as False (6 already False). i=4: is_prime[4]=False, skip. i=5: >√10≈3.16, stop sieve. Result: primes are 2,3,5,7 (is_prime True at indices 2,3,5,7). Step 3: Count pairs from i=2 to 5 (N/2). i=2: N-i=8, is_prime[2]=True, is_prime[8]=False → no pair. i=3: N-i=7, is_prime[3]=True, is_prime[7]=True → pair (3,7). i=4: N-i=6, is_prime[4]=False → no pair. i=5: N-i=5, is_prime[5]=True, is_prime[5]=True → pair (5,5). Count=2. Step 4: Return 2.

Step 1: N=20 even ≥4. Step 2: Sieve up to 20. Initialize is_prime[0..20]=True. Set 0,1 False. i=2: mark 4,6,8,10,12,14,16,18,20 False. i=3: mark 6,9,12,15,18 False (some already False). i=4: False, skip. i=5: >√20≈4.47, stop. Primes: 2,3,5,7,11,13,17,19. Step 3: Count i=2 to 10. i=2: 18 not prime → no. i=3: 17 prime → pair (3,17). i=4: 16 not prime → no. i=5: 15 not prime → no. i=6: 14 not prime → no. i=7: 13 prime → pair (7,13). i=8: 12 not prime → no. i=9: 11 prime → pair (9? 9 not prime) → no, wait is_prime[9]=False from sieve, so no. i=10: 10 not prime → no. Also check i=11? i goes to 10 only (N/2). But we missed i=11? Actually i from 2 to N/2=10, so i=11 not considered. But pair (11,9) is same as (9,11) already counted when i=9? i=9 gave no because 9 not prime. So need to check carefully: i=3: (3,17) yes. i=5: (5,15) no. i=7: (7,13) yes. i=11: not in loop. i=13: not in loop. i=17: not in loop. Also i=2: (2,18) no. i=19: not in loop. Wait, also pair (11,9) but 9 not prime. What about (19,1)? 1 not prime. Let's recount properly: primes are 2,3,5,7,11,13,17,19. Pairs: (3,17), (7,13), (11,9) no, (13,7) duplicate, (17,3) duplicate, (19,1) no. Also (5,15) no. So only 2 pairs? But expected 4. I need to check: For N=20, Goldbach pairs are (3,17), (7,13), (11,9) but 9 not prime, (13,7) duplicate, (17,3) duplicate. That's 2 unique. But known: 20 = 3+17 = 7+13 = 11+9? 9 not prime. 20 = 13+7 duplicate. So only 2. But wait, also (2,18) no, (5,15) no. So output should be 2? Let me verify with standard: Goldbach pairs for 20: 3+17, 7+13. That's 2. But the problem says count ways, order matters? Usually order doesn't matter, so (3,17) and (7,13) are 2 ways. But maybe they count (3,17) and (17,3) as separate? Usually not. Let's assume order doesn't matter. Then for 20, output 2. But I said 4 earlier, that was a mistake. Let me correct: For N=20, output should be 2. But in the trace, I need to show correct counting. Let's redo trace correctly. Step 3: i from 2 to 10. i=2: is_prime[2]=True, is_prime[18]=False → no. i=3: is_prime[3]=True, is_prime[17]=True → count=1. i=4: is_prime[4]=False → no. i=5: is_prime[5]=True, is_prime[15]=False → no. i=6: is_prime[6]=False → no. i=7: is_prime[7]=True, is_prime[13]=True → count=2. i=8: is_prime[8]=False → no. i=9: is_prime[9]=False → no. i=10: is_prime[10]=False → no. Count=2. So output 2. I'll correct the example output to 2.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Generate Primes Using Sieve of Eratosthenes
ladders.desc: Implement Sieve of Eratosthenes to create a boolean array marking primes up to a given limit.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine generates all prime numbers up to a limit N using the Sieve of Eratosthenes algorithm. The intuition is that instead of checking each number individually for primality (which is O(n√n)), we can efficiently mark non-primes by eliminating multiples of each prime starting from 2. The algorithm works by assuming all numbers from 0 to N are prime initially, then marking 0 and 1 as non-prime. For each number i from 2 to √N, if i is still marked as prime, then all multiples of i (starting from i²) are marked as non-prime. This works because any composite number less than N must have a prime factor ≤ √N, so by the time we reach √N, all non-primes have been marked. The time complexity is O(n log log n) due to the harmonic series of primes. Space complexity is O(n) for the boolean array. Connection to L0: This sieve is used in the full problem to precompute primes for quick lookup when counting Goldbach pairs. Common mistakes include: starting marking multiples from i instead of i² (which is less efficient but correct), not optimizing loop to √N, or incorrectly handling array indices. Real code pattern: def sieve_of_eratosthenes(N): is_prime = [True]*(N+1); is_prime[0]=is_prime[1]=False; for i in range(2, int(N**0.5)+1): if is_prime[i]: for j in range(i*i, N+1, i): is_prime[j]=False; return is_prime.

examples.output: [False, False, True, True, False, True, False, True, False, False, False]
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize is_prime array of size 11 (0 to 10) all True. Step 2: Set is_prime[0]=False, is_prime[1]=False. Step 3: Loop i from 2 to √10≈3.16, so i=2,3. i=2: is_prime[2]=True, so mark multiples starting from 2²=4: j=4,6,8,10 → set is_prime[4]=False, is_prime[6]=False, is_prime[8]=False, is_prime[10]=False. i=3: is_prime[3]=True, mark multiples from 3²=9: j=9 → set is_prime[9]=False. i=4 is not considered because 4>√10. Step 4: Result array: indices 0-10: [False, False, True, True, False, True, False, True, False, False, False]. So primes are numbers where True: 2,3,5,7.

examples.output: [False, False, True, True, False, True, False, True, False, False, False, True, False, True, False, False, False, True, False, True, False]
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize is_prime size 21 all True. Step 2: Set index 0 and 1 False. Step 3: Loop i=2 to √20≈4.47, so i=2,3,4. i=2: is_prime[2]=True, mark multiples from 4: 4,6,8,10,12,14,16,18,20 → set False. i=3: is_prime[3]=True, mark multiples from 9: 9,12,15,18 → set False (12,18 already False). i=4: is_prime[4]=False (marked by i=2), so skip. Step 4: Result: [False, False, True, True, False, True, False, True, False, False, False, True, False, True, False, False, False, True, False, True, False]. Primes: 2,3,5,7,11,13,17,19.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Check Prime Pairs for Even Sum
ladders.desc: Given a boolean prime array, count pairs (i, N-i) where both are prime for even N.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves iterating through numbers to find pairs that sum to N, with both numbers being prime, using a precomputed prime array for efficiency. The intuition is that for an even number N, we only need to check i from 2 to N/2 because pairs (i, N-i) and (N-i, i) are symmetric, avoiding double-counting. For each i in this range, if is_prime[i] and is_prime[N-i] are both true, then (i, N-i) is a valid Goldbach pair. This reduces the problem from O(n²) brute force to O(n) after prime generation. The algorithm assumes is_prime array is already computed (e.g., from Sieve in L1). Steps: initialize count=0; for i from 2 to N/2 (inclusive): if is_prime[i] and is_prime[N-i], then increment count; return count. Time complexity is O(n) for the loop, space is O(1) extra. Connection to L1: This uses the is_prime array generated by the sieve. Common mistakes include: starting i from 1 instead of 2 (1 is not prime), going beyond N/2 (which would double-count), or not verifying that N is even. Real code pattern: def count_pairs(N, is_prime): count=0; for i in range(2, N//2+1): if is_prime[i] and is_prime[N-i]: count+=1; return count.

examples.input: N = 10, is_prime = [False, False, True, True, False, True, False, True, False, False, False]
examples.trace: Step 1: N=10, is_prime given for indices 0-10. Step 2: Initialize count=0. Step 3: Loop i from 2 to 5 (10/2). i=2: is_prime[2]=True, is_prime[8]=False → no. i=3: is_prime[3]=True, is_prime[7]=True → count=1. i=4: is_prime[4]=False → no. i=5: is_prime[5]=True, is_prime[5]=True → count=2. Step 4: Return count=2.

examples.input: N = 20, is_prime = [False, False, True, True, False, True, False, True, False, False, False, True, False, True, False, False, False, True, False, True, False]
examples.trace: Step 1: N=20, is_prime given. Step 2: count=0. Step 3: Loop i from 2 to 10. i=2: is_prime[2]=True, is_prime[18]=False → no. i=3: is_prime[3]=True, is_prime[17]=True → count=1. i=4: is_prime[4]=False → no. i=5: is_prime[5]=True, is_prime[15]=False → no. i=6: is_prime[6]=False → no. i=7: is_prime[7]=True, is_prime[13]=True → count=2. i=8: is_prime[8]=False → no. i=9: is_prime[9]=False → no. i=10: is_prime[10]=False → no. Step 4: Return count=2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterate and Check Conditions in a Range
ladders.desc: Loop through a range and apply conditional checks using array lookups.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves iterating over a sequence of numbers and performing conditional checks based on array values. The intuition is that many algorithms require scanning through a range and taking action when certain conditions are met—here, checking if two related indices in an array satisfy a property. Specifically, for a given N, we iterate i from start to end, and for each i, check if both is_prime[i] and is_prime[N-i] are true. This teaches how to use loops with conditions and array indexing. The algorithm: initialize a counter; for i in range(start, end+1): if condition1 and condition2: increment counter; return counter. Time complexity is O(n) for the loop, space is O(1). Connection to L2: This is the loop structure used in the core logic for counting pairs. Common mistakes include: off-by-one errors in range (e.g., using N/2 without +1), incorrect array indexing (e.g., accessing is_prime[N-i] when N-i might be out of bounds, but here N-i is within 0..N), or not initializing the counter. Real code pattern: def iterate_and_check(N, is_prime): count=0; for i in range(2, N//2+1): if is_prime[i] and is_prime[N-i]: count+=1; return count.

examples.input: N = 8, is_prime = [False, False, True, True, False, True, False, True, False], start=2, end=4
examples.trace: Step 1: N=8, is_prime array given. Step 2: count=0. Step 3: Loop i from 2 to 4. i=2: is_prime[2]=True, is_prime[6]=False → condition false. i=3: is_prime[3]=True, is_prime[5]=True → condition true, count=1. i=4: is_prime[4]=False → condition false. Step 4: Return count=1.

examples.input: N = 12, is_prime = [False, False, True, True, False, True, False, True, False, False, False, True, False], start=2, end=6
examples.trace: Step 1: N=12, is_prime given. Step 2: count=0. Step 3: Loop i from 2 to 6. i=2: is_prime[2]=True, is_prime[10]=False → false. i=3: is_prime[3]=True, is_prime[9]=False → false. i=4: is_prime[4]=False → false. i=5: is_prime[5]=True, is_prime[7]=True → true, count=1. i=6: is_prime[6]=False → false. Step 4: Return count=1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Lookup and Boolean Check
ladders.desc: Access array elements and evaluate boolean conditions.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves retrieving values from an array using indices and checking if they meet boolean conditions. The intuition is that arrays allow O(1) access to stored data, and boolean arrays are commonly used to represent properties like primality. Here, we practice accessing is_prime[i] and is_prime[N-i] for a given i and N, then evaluating if both are True using logical AND. This is fundamental to many algorithms that rely on precomputed tables. The operation: given array is_prime, integer N, and integer i, perform: condition = is_prime[i] and is_prime[N-i]. Time complexity is O(1) per access, space is O(1). Connection to L3: This condition check is used within the loop of the building block. Common mistakes include: accessing indices out of array bounds (e.g., if i or N-i is negative or greater than array length), misunderstanding boolean values (e.g., treating 0/1 as True/False incorrectly), or not using proper logical operators. Real code pattern: def check_pair(i, N, is_prime): return is_prime[i] and is_prime[N-i].

examples.input: i = 3, N = 10, is_prime = [False, False, True, True, False, True, False, True, False, False, False]
examples.trace: Step 1: Access is_prime[3]: value at index 3 is True. Step 2: Compute N-i = 10-3 = 7. Access is_prime[7]: value at index 7 is True. Step 3: Evaluate condition: True and True = True. Step 4: Return True.

examples.input: i = 4, N = 10, is_prime = [False, False, True, True, False, True, False, True, False, False, False]
examples.trace: Step 1: Access is_prime[4]: value at index 4 is False. Step 2: Compute N-i = 10-4 = 6. Access is_prime[6]: value at index 6 is False. Step 3: Evaluate condition: False and False = False. Step 4: Return False.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Boolean Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Create and access boolean arrays using indices.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces boolean arrays (lists of True/False values) and how to access them using indices. A boolean array is a data structure that stores boolean values at consecutive indices, typically used to represent properties for a set of items—for example, is_prime[i] indicates whether number i is prime. The intuition is that arrays allow efficient O(1) lookup by index, making them ideal for precomputed results. To create a boolean array of size n+1 initialized to True: is_prime = [True]*(n+1). To set specific indices: is_prime[0] = False; is_prime[1] = False. To access value at index i: value = is_prime[i]. This is fundamental because the Sieve of Eratosthenes and the pair checking rely heavily on such arrays. Time complexity for access is O(1), space is O(n) for the array. Connection to L4: Boolean arrays are used in the array lookup operation. Common mistakes include: off-by-one errors in array size (e.g., size n instead of n+1 for indices 0..n), incorrect initialization, or misunderstanding zero-based indexing. Real code pattern: def create_boolean_array(n): arr = [True]*(n+1); arr[0]=False; arr[1]=False; return arr.

examples.trace: Step 1: Create array of size 5+1=6, all True: [True, True, True, True, True, True]. Step 2: Set index 0 to False: [False, True, True, True, True, True]. Step 3: Set index 1 to False: [False, False, True, True, True, True]. Step 4: Return the array.

examples.output: [False, False, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True]
examples.trace: Step 1: Create array of size 10+1=11, all True: [True]*11. Step 2: Set index 0 False: [False, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True]. Step 3: Set index 1 False: [False, False, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True]. Step 4: Return array.

testCases.expected: [False, False, True, True, True, True, True]
[False, False, True, True, True, True, True, True]
[False, False, True, True, True, True, True, True, True]
[False, False, True, True, True, True, True, True, True, True]

approaches.name: Sieve with Two Pointer O(n log log n)
approaches.summary: Generate a list of primes up to N using the Sieve of Eratosthenes, then use two pointers (one at start, one at end) to count pairs that sum to N. This avoids redundant iterations over non-prime numbers.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Goldbach Pairs Using Sieve with Two Pointer
ladders.desc: Complete solution to count ways to write even N as sum of two primes using Sieve of Eratosthenes and two-pointer technique.
ladders.explanation: This solution combines prime generation with efficient pair counting. First, we generate all primes up to N using the Sieve of Eratosthenes, which marks non-prime numbers by iterating from 2 to √N. This creates a boolean array where isPrime[i] = True if i is prime. Then, we extract all primes from this array into a list. Next, we use two pointers: left starts at the beginning of the prime list, right starts at the end. While left ≤ right, we calculate sum = primes[left] + primes[right]. If sum == N, we count this pair and move both pointers inward. If sum < N, we move left pointer right to increase the sum. If sum > N, we move right pointer left to decrease the sum. This works because the prime list is sorted, allowing us to find all pairs in O(p) time where p is the number of primes ≤ N. The overall time complexity is O(n log log n) dominated by the sieve, with O(n) space for the boolean array. The intuition is that by generating primes efficiently and using sorted two-pointer search, we avoid O(n²) brute force checking. Connection to L1: This integrates the prime generation from L1 with the two-pointer counting logic. Common mistakes include not handling N=2 (no pairs), forgetting that (p, p) counts when p = N/2 is prime, and off-by-one errors in pointer movement. Real code pattern: def countGoldbachPairs(N): if N < 4: return 0; isPrime = [True]*(N+1); isPrime[0]=isPrime[1]=False; for i in range(2, int(N**0.5)+1): if isPrime[i]: for j in range(i*i, N+1, i): isPrime[j]=False; primes = [i for i in range(2, N+1) if isPrime[i]]; left, right, count = 0, len(primes)-1, 0; while left <= right: s = primes[left] + primes[right]; if s == N: count += 1; left += 1; right -= 1; elif s < N: left += 1; else: right -= 1; return count.

examples.trace: N=10 (even). Step 1: Generate primes up to 10 using sieve. Initialize isPrime[0..10] = [F,F,T,T,T,T,T,T,T,T,T]. Mark multiples: i=2 → mark 4,6,8,10 → [F,F,T,T,F,T,F,T,F,T,F]; i=3 → mark 9 → [F,F,T,T,F,T,F,T,F,F,F]; i=4 not prime. Primes list = [2,3,5,7]. Step 2: Two-pointer counting. left=0 (2), right=3 (7), sum=9 < 10 → left=1; left=1 (3), right=3 (7), sum=10 == 10 → count=1, left=2, right=2; left=2 (5), right=2 (5), sum=10 == 10 → count=2, left=3, right=1 (stop). Return count=2. Pairs: (3,7) and (5,5).

N=20. Step 1: Sieve up to 20. isPrime after marking: [F,F,T,T,F,T,F,T,F,F,F,T,F,T,F,F,F,T,F,T,F]. Primes list = [2,3,5,7,11,13,17,19]. Step 2: Two-pointer. left=0 (2), right=7 (19) sum=21 > 20 → right=6; left=0 (2), right=6 (17) sum=19 < 20 → left=1; left=1 (3), right=6 (17) sum=20 == 20 → count=1, left=2, right=5; left=2 (5), right=5 (13) sum=18 < 20 → left=3; left=3 (7), right=5 (13) sum=20 == 20 → count=2, left=4, right=4; left=4 (11), right=4 (11) sum=22 > 20 → right=3 (stop). Return count=2? Wait, check: Actually pairs for 20 are (3,17), (7,13), and (13,7), (17,3) but we count unordered, so (3,17) and (7,13) only? Let's trace correctly: After count=2 at (7,13), left=4, right=4, then left=4 (11), right=4 (11) sum=22 > 20 → right=3, left=4 > right=3 stop. So count=2, but expected 4? Let's recalc: Goldbach pairs for 20: (3,17), (7,13), (13,7), (17,3) but algorithm counts unordered pairs, so (3,17) and (7,13) give count=2. But problem says 'Count ways to write even N as sum of two primes' typically means unordered pairs, so (3,17) and (7,13) are 2 ways. However, test expects 4? Let's verify: Actually, common interpretation is distinct pairs (a,b) where a ≤ b and both prime, so (3,17) and (7,13) only. But let's check with known: 20 = 3+17 = 7+13, so 2 ways. So output should be 2. But I said 4 earlier, correction: output=2. Trace update: Final count=2.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Generate Primes Using Sieve of Eratosthenes
ladders.desc: Implement Sieve of Eratosthenes to generate all prime numbers up to a given limit N.
ladders.explanation: The Sieve of Eratosthenes is an ancient algorithm for finding all primes up to N efficiently. The intuition is to iteratively mark multiples of each prime as non-prime, starting from 2. By the time we reach a number, if it hasn't been marked, it must be prime. This works because any composite number has a prime factor ≤ its square root, so we only need to sieve up to √N. Step-by-step: 1. Create a boolean array isPrime of size N+1, initialize all to True except isPrime[0] and isPrime[1] = False. 2. For i from 2 to √N: if isPrime[i] is True, then for j from i*i to N with step i: set isPrime[j] = False. 3. After the loop, all indices i where isPrime[i] is True are primes. Time complexity is O(n log log n) due to harmonic series of primes, space is O(n). Connection to L0: This prime list feeds into the two-pointer counting. Common mistakes include starting j from i*2 instead of i*i (optimization), not handling N<2, and using O(n²) naive checking. Real code pattern: def sieve(N): if N < 2: return []; isPrime = [True]*(N+1); isPrime[0]=isPrime[1]=False; for i in range(2, int(N**0.5)+1): if isPrime[i]: for j in range(i*i, N+1, i): isPrime[j]=False; return [i for i in range(2, N+1) if isPrime[i]].

examples.trace: N=10. Step 1: isPrime = [F, F, T, T, T, T, T, T, T, T, T]. Step 2: i=2 (≤√10≈3.16), isPrime[2]=True → mark multiples: j from 4 to 10 step 2: set isPrime[4]=F, isPrime[6]=F, isPrime[8]=F, isPrime[10]=F. Now isPrime = [F,F,T,T,F,T,F,T,F,T,F]. i=3, isPrime[3]=True → mark multiples: j from 9 to 10 step 3: set isPrime[9]=F. isPrime = [F,F,T,T,F,T,F,T,F,F,F]. i=4 > √10, stop. Step 3: Collect primes: indices 2,3,5,7 are True → list [2,3,5,7].

N=20. Step 1: isPrime size 21 all True except 0,1 False. Step 2: i=2 (≤√20≈4.47), mark 4,6,8,10,12,14,16,18,20 False. i=3, mark 9,15 False (6,12,18 already False). i=4 not prime (False), skip. i=5 > √20 stop. Step 3: Primes: 2,3,5,7,11,13,17,19.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Two Pointer Technique on Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Use two pointers to find all pairs in a sorted array that sum to a target value.
ladders.explanation: The two-pointer technique efficiently finds pairs in a sorted array that sum to a target. Intuition: Since the array is sorted, we can use one pointer at the start (left) and one at the end (right). If their sum equals the target, we found a pair and move both inward. If sum is less than target, moving left pointer right increases the sum (since array is ascending). If sum is greater, moving right pointer left decreases the sum. This works in O(n) time vs O(n²) brute force. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize left=0, right=len(arr)-1, count=0. 2. While left ≤ right: calculate sum = arr[left] + arr[right]. 3. If sum == target: count++, left++, right--. 4. If sum < target: left++. 5. If sum > target: right--. 6. Return count. Time O(n), space O(1). Connection to L0: This counts prime pairs after sieve provides sorted prime list. Common mistakes include using left < right instead of left ≤ right (misses pair where left==right and arr[left]*2==target), and not handling duplicates correctly if array has duplicates (but primes are unique). Real code pattern: def twoPointerSum(arr, target): left, right, count = 0, len(arr)-1, 0; while left <= right: s = arr[left] + arr[right]; if s == target: count += 1; left += 1; right -= 1; elif s < target: left += 1; else: right -= 1; return count.

examples.trace: arr sorted, target=10. left=0 (2), right=3 (7), sum=9 < 10 → left=1; left=1 (3), right=3 (7), sum=10 == 10 → count=1, left=2, right=2; left=2 (5), right=2 (5), sum=10 == 10 → count=2, left=3, right=1 (stop). Return 2. Pairs: (3,7) and (5,5).

left=0 (1), right=4 (5), sum=6 == 6 → count=1, left=1, right=3; left=1 (2), right=3 (4), sum=6 == 6 → count=2, left=2, right=2; left=2 (3), right=2 (3), sum=6 == 6? But left ≤ right, so check: sum=6 == 6 → count=3? Wait, but (3,3) uses same element twice, if allowed? Typically for distinct elements, but here array has unique values, so (3,3) not valid since need two different elements. In Goldbach, primes are distinct indices but can have same value if N/2 is prime. For this generic two-pointer, we should count only when left < right for distinct elements. Let's clarify: In Goldbach, we use left ≤ right to allow (p,p) when p = N/2. For this example, if we want distinct indices, use left < right. So trace correction: left=0 (1), right=4 (5) sum=6 → count=1, left=1, right=3; left=1 (2), right=3 (4) sum=6 → count=2, left=2, right=2; now left=2, right=2, but left < right false, stop. Return 2. Pairs: (1,5) and (2,4).

ladders.title: Building Block — Check if a Number is Prime
ladders.desc: Determine whether a given integer n is a prime number.
ladders.explanation: A prime number is a natural number greater than 1 that has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself. The intuition for checking is to test divisibility by integers from 2 up to √n, because if n has a divisor d > √n, then n/d < √n would have been found earlier. Step-by-step: 1. If n < 2, return False. 2. For i from 2 to √n (inclusive): if n % i == 0, return False. 3. If no divisors found, return True. Time O(√n), space O(1). Connection to L1: This is the foundational check used in sieve marking. Common mistakes include checking up to n-1 (inefficient), not handling n=2 correctly (2 is prime), and off-by-one in range. Real code pattern: def isPrime(n): if n < 2: return False; for i in range(2, int(n**0.5)+1): if n % i == 0: return False; return True.

examples.trace: n=7. Step 1: n≥2, proceed. Step 2: i from 2 to √7≈2.64, so i=2: 7%2=1 ≠0; i=3 > √7 stop. Step 3: No divisors found, return True.
n=10. Step 1: n≥2. Step 2: i from 2 to √10≈3.16, i=2: 10%2=0 → return False immediately.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterate and Mark Multiples in an Array
ladders.desc: Given an array and a starting index, mark all multiples of that index as False.
ladders.explanation: This operation is a core step in the Sieve of Eratosthenes. The intuition is that if an index i represents a prime number, then all multiples of i (i*2, i*3, ...) are composite and should be marked as non-prime. To optimize, we start marking from i*i because smaller multiples like i*2, i*3, ..., i*(i-1) would have been marked by smaller primes. Step-by-step: Given a boolean array isPrime of size N+1 and an integer i (≥2), for j from i*i to N with step i: set isPrime[j] = False. Time O(N/i) for each i, space O(1). Connection to L3: This uses the concept that multiples of a prime are not prime. Common mistakes include starting j from i*2 (less efficient), not checking if i is within array bounds, and step size errors. Real code pattern: for j in range(i*i, N+1, i): isPrime[j] = False.

examples.trace: Initial isPrime for indices 0..10: [F,F,T,T,T,T,T,T,T,T,T]. i=2, start j from i*i=4 to 10 step 2: j=4 → set isPrime[4]=F; j=6 → set isPrime[6]=F; j=8 → set isPrime[8]=F; j=10 → set isPrime[10]=F. Result: [F,F,T,T,F,T,F,T,F,T,F].
Initial isPrime: [F,F,T,T,F,T,F,T,F,T,F]. i=3, j from 9 to 10 step 3: j=9 → set isPrime[9]=F; j=12 >10 stop. Result: [F,F,T,T,F,T,F,T,F,F,F].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Prime Numbers
ladders.desc: Learn what prime numbers are and how to identify them.
ladders.explanation: Prime numbers are the building blocks of integers. A prime number is a natural number greater than 1 that cannot be formed by multiplying two smaller natural numbers. In other words, it has exactly two distinct positive divisors: 1 and itself. Examples: 2, 3, 5, 7, 11. Numbers greater than 1 that are not prime are called composite (e.g., 4, 6, 8, 9). The number 1 is neither prime nor composite. Intuition: Primes are 'atomic' in multiplication; every integer can be uniquely factored into primes (Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic). To check if a number n is prime, we test if it is divisible by any integer from 2 to n-1, but efficiently only up to √n because if n = a*b, then at least one factor ≤ √n. Step-by-step for beginners: 1. If n is less than 2, it's not prime. 2. For each number i from 2 to n-1 (or √n for efficiency): if n divided by i has remainder 0, then n is composite. 3. If no such i found, n is prime. Time O(n) naive, O(√n) optimized. Connection to L4: This concept underlies marking multiples in the sieve. Common mistakes: thinking 1 is prime, or that even numbers >2 can be prime (only 2 is even prime). Real code pattern for naive check: def isPrime(n): if n < 2: return False; for i in range(2, n): if n % i == 0: return False; return True.

examples.trace: n=5. Step 1: 5 ≥ 2, so proceed. Step 2: Check divisibility: 5%2=1 ≠0; 5%3=2 ≠0; 5%4=1 ≠0. Step 3: No divisors found, so 5 is prime.
n=6. Step 1: 6 ≥ 2. Step 2: Check: 6%2=0 → divisible by 2, so 6 is composite. Stop early.
